claim: Context engineering evolved from simple rules files to a combination of reusable instructions, context interfaces (skills, MCP servers), and just-in-time lazy loading of context to avoid filling up token limits
actionable: Design agent workflows with progressive context loading: start with descriptions of skills, load full details only when the agent decides to use them
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: Skills help modularize context by enabling folder-based organization instead of one monolithic rules file, allowing selective just-in-time loading by the LLM
actionable: Organize agent instructions into skill folders (e.g., React components, AWS logging) that can be selectively loaded by the agent based on task context
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: LLM context windows fill quickly even at session start due to system prompts, skill descriptions, and tool lists; context management tools show Claude Code allocates 15% of context to these overhead items immediately
actionable: Monitor context usage in your coding agent settings regularly; prioritize high-ROI skills and prune or archive unused context to maintain efficiency
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: Sub-agents allow domain agents to spawn research or review agents in separate contexts to avoid token overload; researchers often spawn sub-agents automatically to search codebases without bloating the main session
actionable: Use sub-agents to isolate high-token tasks (codebase research, code review) from your main agent session; configure auto-spawning for research tasks
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: Risk assessment for AI task suitability combines probability (AI gets it wrong), impact (criticality: PoC vs. production outage), and detectability (strength of feedback loops); this determines appropriate supervision level
actionable: Before using AI on a task, assess: How confident am I in my requirements? How critical is this system? Do I have good tests/logs to detect failures? Use these to choose supervision level
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: The 'lethal trifecta' security risk occurs when an agent has exposure to untrusted content, access to private data, and ability to externally communicate; agents with email read+send + GitHub push access represent this risk
actionable: Audit agent permissions: avoid granting simultaneous read+execute+exfil capabilities; sandbox agents with untrusted inputs; implement rate-limiting on external comms
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: AI coding costs have risen from ~$0.12 per 100 lines (early 2024) to $380/day average (summer 2025) because agents now research, plan, implement, test, lint, review, and iterate—not just autocomplete
actionable: Budget AI coding costs per agent per developer: ~$380/day = ~$91k annual (at summer 2025 rates); explore rate limiting, batching, and agent pruning to reduce spend
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: Harness engineering combines feed-forward (context, principles, CLIs, language servers) and feedback loops (static analysis, logs, tests); built-in structural tests amplify trust without requiring custom tooling
actionable: Use Dependency Cruiser, ArchUnit, or Spring Modulith to enforce architectural constraints and provide agents with deterministic feedback before human review
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: Structural tests can be enhanced with custom error messages that hint to the agent how to fix violations; e.g., 'File exceeds 500 lines = smell for design problem, refactor' steers agent toward intent not just compliance
actionable: Write linting error messages as agent guidance, not just constraints; explain the *why* behind limits so agents internalize the principle
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: CLIs, bootstrap scripts, and language server integrations (e.g., IntelliJ refactoring) are feed-forward CPU-based tools that increase probability agent generates correct code before feedback loops kick in
actionable: Give agents access to your existing CLI tools, code mods, and IDE refactoring APIs; this shifts from text-diff corrections to deterministic transformations
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: OpenAI team working on 5-month greenfield project reported entropy and drift even with structured testing and harness; 'garbage collection' (continuous agent cleanup passes) helped maintain code quality
actionable: Plan for ongoing agent-driven code maintenance: schedule periodic 'cleanup' passes with agents to refactor and remove accumulated technical debt
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy

claim: Reducing human supervision increases cognitive overload: anecdotes show experienced engineers suffering burnout from parallel multi-session work; pressure from leadership to move faster causes corner-cutting
actionable: Monitor team health when rolling out unsupervised agents; provide review tools and rate-limit agent autonomy by project risk; don't conflate velocity with sustainable delivery
source_title: The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy
